outcome.headline: Agent wrote a partial answer (missing the parity-piecewise solution family), then the post-`task_complete` reconfirmation LLM call hung for 555s, triggering AgentTimeoutError before the verifier ever ran.
outcome.what_verifier_checked: LLM judge would have compared answer.txt against the canonical solutions: (1) f(n)=0 if n even, any perfect square if n odd; (2) f(n)=n^2 for every integer n.
outcome.what_agent_produced: answer.txt containing 'f(n) = 0 for all n in Z\nf(n) = n^2 for all n in Z' — captures the f(n)=n^2 family and only the trivial case of the first family, missing the general 'any perfect square on odd n' construction; verifier never executed due to AgentTimeoutError.
outcome.exact_failure_quote: AgentTimeoutError: Agent execution timed out after 600.0 seconds

failure_modes.name: Incomplete solution family — assumed f must be a polynomial
failure_modes.description: In the reasoning chain the agent proves S_a(x) must be affine and therefore f must be a polynomial of degree ≤ 2, concluding only f=0 and f(x)=x^2 are solutions. This excludes the second canonical family in which f(n)=0 on even n and f(n) equals an arbitrary perfect square on odd n — a non-polynomial integer function that also satisfies the constraint. The polynomial-only reduction is the logical error that loses the more general family.
failure_modes.evidence_quote: So only solutions are f(x) = 0 and f(x) = x^2.

counterfactual.X: Test the parity-piecewise candidate f(n)=0 for even n, f(n)=s_n^2 for odd n directly against P(a,b) before committing to the polynomial reduction.
counterfactual.Y: Including the 'f(n)=0 on even, perfect square on odd' family in answer.txt would have matched the ideal_answer's first listed family and produced full credit from the LLM judge.

failure_modes.name: Premature task_complete before confirmation
failure_modes.description: The agent set task_complete=true in its first and only response after a single megablob reasoning step, leaving no budget to respond to the confirmation prompt and forfeiting any chance to revise the incomplete answer
failure_modes.evidence_quote: Are you sure you want to mark the task as complete? This will trigger your solution to be graded and you won't be able to make any further corrections.

failure_modes.name: Confirmation round never reached due to budget exhaustion
failure_modes.description: Agent invoked mark_task_complete in step 2; harness replied 'Are you sure you want to mark the task as complete? ... include task_complete: true in your JSON response again.' but the next LLM call could not complete within the remaining ~45s of the 600s budget.
failure_modes.evidence_quote: Are you sure you want to mark the task as complete? This will trigger your solution to be graded and you won't be able to make any further corrections. If so, include "task_complete": true in your JSON response again.

counterfactual.X: If a second, fast LLM call had been able to return within the budget, the agent would have re-issued task_complete:true and the verifier would have judged the (partially correct) answer.
counterfactual.Y: Trial would have at least reached verification; outcome would depend on LLM-judge tolerance to a partial solution mentioning f(n)=n^2.

failure_modes.name: Completion protocol not confirmed before timeout
failure_modes.description: After writing and previewing the answer, the harness required a second explicit completion confirmation, but the run timed out before the agent supplied it.
failure_modes.evidence_quote: Are you sure you want to mark the task as complete? This will trigger your solution to be graded and you won't be able to make any further corrections. If so, include "task_complete": true in your JSON response again.

failure_modes.name: Incomplete characterization of the solution family
failure_modes.description: Agent's algebraic derivation concluded the only solutions are f≡0 and f(n)=n^2, missing the larger family 'f(n)=0 on evens, any perfect square on odds' explicitly listed as the canonical ideal_answer alongside f(n)=n^2. The polynomial-only argument silently assumed f is a polynomial.
failure_modes.evidence_quote: So I think the only solutions are indeed f(n) = 0 and f(n) = n^2.

failure_modes.name: Budget exhausted before confirmation
failure_modes.description: The sole agent LLM call consumed ~555s of the 600s agent timeout (17268 completion tokens of reasoning) before emitting shell commands; harbor then raised AgentTimeoutError during the next LLM interaction while the harness asked for task-complete confirmation.
failure_modes.evidence_quote: Agent execution timed out after 600.0 seconds

failure_modes.name: Single monolithic reasoning step consumed nearly the entire wall-clock budget
failure_modes.description: Step 2 emitted 17,268 completion tokens and ran from 02:14:00 to 02:23:20 (≈555s of one 600s budget). All exploration, case-analysis, and polynomial-coefficient algebra were poured into one chat turn instead of being broken into smaller verifiable steps with the python interpreter the harness provides.

failure_modes.name: Single oversized reasoning step exhausted the time budget

outcome.exact_failure_quote: Agent execution timed out after 600.0 seconds

failure_modes.name: Incomplete mathematical solution set
failure_modes.description: The answer omitted the canonical family where f(n)=0 on even integers and f(n) can be any perfect square on odd integers. The agent reasoned that only the zero function and f(n)=n^2 work, so the produced answer was mathematically incomplete even apart from the timeout.
failure_modes.evidence_quote: Through analysis, I determined the solutions are f(n) = 0 and f(n) = n^2.

counterfactual.X: Recognize that f(2a)=0 with square-valued odd inputs satisfies the condition and is not only the all-zero function.
counterfactual.Y: Write both the piecewise family f(even)=0, f(odd)=any perfect square, and f(n)=n^2.

failure_modes.name: Completion confirmation not satisfied
failure_modes.description: After writing answer.txt, the harness asked for explicit confirmation before grading. The run then timed out without sending the required second task_complete confirmation, leaving verifier_result null in result.json.
failure_modes.evidence_quote: Are you sure you want to mark the task as complete? This will trigger your solution to be graded and you won't be able to make any further corrections. If so, include "task_complete": true in your JSON response again.

reward_hacking.evidence: The trajectory shows ordinary mathematical reasoning and writing /workspace/answer.txt; there is no attempt to manipulate the judge, logs, or tests.

issues: The verifier is an open-ended LLM judge over /workspace/answer.txt, so adversarial answer text could try to influence grading.
task_quality.structural_hackability_notes: llm_judge.py inserts the predicted answer directly into an LLM grading prompt and asks for binary_score, which is structurally susceptible to prompt-injection style answers.

outcome.headline: The agent wrote an incomplete set of functions and then timed out before confirming completion.
outcome.what_verifier_checked: The task verifier runs an LLM judge over /workspace/answer.txt against ground_truths.json, whose ideal answer includes both f(n)=n^2 and the family f(even)=0 with arbitrary perfect-square values on odd integers.
outcome.what_agent_produced:
f(n) = 0 for all n in Z
f(n) = n^2 for all n in Z
outcome.exact_failure_quote: Agent execution timed out after 600.0 seconds

failure_modes.name: Incomplete mathematical characterization
failure_modes.description: The agent omitted the nonconstant parity family where all even inputs map to 0 and odd inputs map to arbitrary perfect squares, reducing that family to only the zero function.
failure_modes.evidence_quote: I determined the solutions are f(n) = 0 and f(n) = n^2.

failure_modes.name: Completion protocol not confirmed before timeout
failure_modes.description: After writing and previewing the answer, the harness required a second explicit completion confirmation, but the run timed out before the agent supplied it.
failure_modes.evidence_quote: Are you sure you want to mark the task as complete? This will trigger your solution to be graded and you won't be able to make any further corrections. If so, include "task_complete": true in your JSON response again.

reward_hacking.evidence: The trajectory shows direct mathematical reasoning and writing /workspace/answer.txt; there is no evidence of reading hidden answers, tampering with tests, or manipulating the verifier.

task_quality.structural_hackability_notes: The verifier is an open-ended LLM equivalence judge over answer.txt; no structural hack was used in the trajectory.

outcome.headline: The agent produced an incomplete function list, then timed out instead of confirming completion after the harness prompt.
outcome.what_verifier_checked: The verifier runs an LLM-judge ensemble over /workspace/answer.txt against ground_truths.json. The target includes the family f(even)=0 with arbitrary perfect-square values on odds, plus f(n)=n^2. No ./trial/test_stdout.txt was present, and result.json has verifier_result null because the agent timed out.
outcome.what_agent_produced: It wrote only two functions: f(n)=0 for all n and f(n)=n^2 for all n, omitting the arbitrary odd-square family; it then received the completion-confirmation prompt but did not send the required follow-up task_complete response before timeout.
outcome.exact_failure_quote: Agent execution timed out after 600.0 seconds

failure_modes.name: Incomplete mathematical characterization
failure_modes.description: The final answer omitted the valid family where all even inputs map to 0 and odd inputs map to arbitrary perfect squares. The agent treated the zero function as the only member of that family and concluded only two functions existed.
failure_modes.evidence_quote:
f(n) = 0 for all n in Z
f(n) = n^2 for all n in Z

counterfactual.X: At step 2, write the complete answer including f(n)=0 for even n and arbitrary perfect-square values for odd n, plus f(n)=n^2.
counterfactual.Y: The answer content would match the verifier target instead of being only a partial subset.

failure_modes.name: Failed completion confirmation
failure_modes.description: After invoking mark_task_complete, the harness explicitly asked for a second confirmation with task_complete true. The agent did not provide that follow-up before the 600-second execution timeout.
failure_modes.evidence_quote: Are you sure you want to mark the task as complete? This will trigger your solution to be graded and you won't be able to make any further corrections. If so, include "task_complete": true in your JSON response again.

counterfactual.X: Only sending the missing confirmation would have avoided the timeout.
counterfactual.Y: The submitted answer would still have been incomplete, so this alone would not make the trial pass.

reward_hacking.evidence: The trajectory shows mathematical reasoning, writing /workspace/answer.txt, and catting it back. There is no attempt to inspect verifier files or manipulate grading.

issues: The verifier is an LLM judge that directly embeds the predicted answer in the grading prompt, which is structurally more prompt-injection susceptible than a deterministic checker.
task_quality.structural_hackability_notes: llm_judge.py formats the predicted answer directly into OPEN_ENDED_GRADING_PROMPT and asks models for binary_score JSON. A malicious answer could try to influence the judge prompt, although this trial did not attempt that.

outcome.headline: The agent spent nearly the full 600s budget on one reasoning-heavy turn, then submitted an incomplete classification (only f≡0 and f(n)=n²) while omitting the piecewise even/odd solution family required by the reference answer.
outcome.what_verifier_checked: tests/llm_judge.py reads /workspace/answer.txt and grades it against ground_truths.json ideal_answer via an LLM-judge ensemble (binary_score → reward in /logs/verifier/reward.json); tests/test.sh runs that judge.
outcome.what_agent_produced: Wrote /workspace/answer.txt with two lines: f(n)=0 for all n∈ℤ and f(n)=n² for all n∈ℤ, then marked task_complete; harness timed out before a follow-up confirmation turn.

failure_modes.name: Incomplete solution classification
failure_modes.description: In step 2 the agent concluded and submitted only the constant-zero and global n² functions, missing the reference’s piecewise family (0 on evens, arbitrary perfect square on odds) that completes “all functions.”
failure_modes.evidence_quote: only solutions are f=0 and f(x)=x^2

failure_modes.name: Reasoning budget exhaustion
failure_modes.description: The sole agent turn generated ~17k completion tokens over ~555s (per result.json api_request_times_msec), leaving no time to refine or confirm completion before AgentTimeoutError at 600s.
failure_modes.evidence_quote: Agent execution timed out after 600.0 seconds

counterfactual.X: Write the identified candidates to /workspace/answer.txt early, then iterate or confirm completion within the 600s agent budget.
counterfactual.Y: Emitted a single marathon reasoning block (~17268 completion tokens) before the first shell write.

issues: Grading relies on an LLM-judge ensemble (llm_verifier) comparing free-text math answers to a LaTeX ideal_answer, which can be format-sensitive and non-deterministic across judge models.
task_quality.structural_hackability_notes: An agent can paraphrase keywords from the ideal_answer (e.g., even/odd, perfect square, n^2) without a correct full classification and still obtain binary_score=true from the LLM judge.

outcome.headline: The agent submitted an incomplete characterization (only global zero and n²) and then hit the 600s agent timeout during the harness confirmation turn.
outcome.what_verifier_checked: test.sh runs llm_judge.py, which reads /workspace/answer.txt and uses an LLM ensemble to compare the prediction against ideal_answer in ground_truths.json (eval_mode llm_verifier, binary_score averaged to reward).
outcome.what_agent_produced: /workspace/answer.txt containing exactly: f(n)=0 for all n in Z and f(n)=n² for all n in Z.

failure_modes.name: Incomplete solution family set
failure_modes.description: After a long internal proof, the agent concluded and wrote only f≡0 and f(n)=n², omitting the ground-truth piecewise family (0 on evens, arbitrary perfect square on odds) documented in ground_truths.json.
failure_modes.evidence_quote: Thus the only solutions are f=0 and f(x)=x^2.

failure_modes.name: Monolithic reasoning burst timeout
failure_modes.description: Step 2 issued one LLM completion (~555s API time, 17268 output tokens) that consumed nearly the full 600s agent budget; execution then raised AgentTimeoutError during the next LLM interaction before the confirmation episode finished.
failure_modes.evidence_quote: Agent execution timed out after 600.0 seconds

issues: Grading depends on an LLM judge ensemble rather than a deterministic checker, so borderline answer equivalence is model-dependent.
ideal_answer and reference solution use LaTeX with corrupted control sequences (e.g. ext instead of \text), which can add judge noise.
task_quality.structural_hackability_notes: An LLM verifier comparing free-text answers to a prose ideal_answer can be satisfied by plausible but incomplete function lists without a formal uniqueness proof.

outcome.headline: The agent wrote only f≡0 and f(n)=n², omitting the even/odd piecewise solution family required by the ground truth, and failed LLM-judge grading.
outcome.what_verifier_checked: tests/llm_judge.py reads /workspace/answer.txt and grades it against ideal_answer in tests/ground_truths.json via an LLM-judge ensemble (eval_mode llm_verifier), writing reward to /logs/verifier/reward.json (tests/test.sh:6-10).
outcome.what_agent_produced: Two-line plain-text answer in /workspace/answer.txt: f(n)=0 for all n∈ℤ and f(n)=n² for all n∈ℤ, then marked the task complete.

failure_modes.name: Omitted piecewise solutions
failure_modes.description: In step 2 the agent concluded and submitted only the constant-zero and quadratic solutions, explicitly ruling out other families, while the canonical ideal_answer also requires f(n)=0 on evens and an arbitrary perfect square on odds.
failure_modes.evidence_quote: Thus the only solutions are f=0 and f(x)=x^2.

counterfactual.X: Enumerate all solution families from the ground truth, including the even/odd piecewise form with arbitrary perfect squares on odd integers, and write that complete set to /workspace/answer.txt.
counterfactual.Y: Declared that only f(n)=0 and f(n)=n² work and echoed just those two lines to /workspace/answer.txt.

failure_modes.name: Skipped answer validation
failure_modes.description: After writing answer.txt the agent immediately called mark_task_complete without running any local check, sample substitution, or comparison against the stated grading requirement.
failure_modes.evidence_quote: Are you sure you want to mark the task as complete?

issues: Pass/fail depends on an LLM-judge ensemble comparing free-form answer.txt to a LaTeX ideal_answer, which can vary on notation and completeness wording.
The piecewise even/odd solution family is easy to miss if an agent stops after finding f≡0 and f(n)=n².
task_quality.structural_hackability_notes: llm_verifier mode grades semantic equivalence via model prompts rather than symbolic or programmatic equality, so persuasive but incomplete prose can sometimes pass.

outcome.headline: The agent spent ~555s on one reasoning-heavy LLM turn, wrote an incomplete answer omitting the piecewise even/odd solution family, then timed out before confirming task completion.
outcome.what_verifier_checked: tests/test.sh runs llm_judge.py, which reads /workspace/answer.txt and grades it against ideal_answer in ground_truths.json via an LLM ensemble (eval_mode llm_verifier, binary_score).
outcome.what_agent_produced: /workspace/answer.txt with two lines: f(n)=0 for all n and f(n)=n^2 for all n (no piecewise f(0) on evens / arbitrary perfect square on odds).

failure_modes.name: Omitted piecewise solution family
failure_modes.description: After lengthy functional-equation analysis, the agent concluded only f≡0 and f(n)=n^2, and wrote that pair to answer.txt. The canonical solution also includes f(n)=0 for even n and any perfect square for odd n.
failure_modes.evidence_quote: Thus the only solutions are f=0 and f(x)=x^2.

failure_modes.name: Reasoning burst caused timeout
failure_modes.description: Episode 1 consumed ~555s generating 17,268 completion tokens of internal reasoning before shell commands; episode 2 hung on the next LLM call until the 600s agent timeout, leaving the harness confirmation prompt unanswered.
failure_modes.evidence_quote: Agent execution timed out after 600.0 seconds

counterfactual.X: Emit a short follow-up JSON with task_complete:true promptly after the confirmation prompt instead of starting another long LLM completion.
counterfactual.Y: The second LLM request ran until AgentTimeoutError with no further agent output after mark_task_complete.

task_quality.structural_hackability_notes: Open-ended math grading via LLM ensemble (llm_judge.py) can accept paraphrases or reject correct-but-unusual notation; not a task defect for omnimath.

outcome.headline: The agent spent nearly the full 600s budget on one reasoning burst, wrote only f(n)=0 and f(n)=n^2 to answer.txt, omitted the required piecewise even/odd solution family, and timed out before a follow-up turn.
outcome.what_verifier_checked: tests/llm_judge.py reads /workspace/answer.txt and grades it with eval_mode llm_verifier against ideal_answer in tests/ground_truths.json via an LLM-judge ensemble (reward written to /logs/verifier/reward.json).
outcome.what_agent_produced: In step 2, wrote /workspace/answer.txt containing exactly: "f(n) = 0 for all n in Z" and "f(n) = n^2 for all n in Z", then called mark_task_complete.

failure_modes.name: Missed piecewise solution family
failure_modes.description: The ground truth requires two solution classes: f(n)=n^2 for all n, and f(n)=0 on even integers with any perfect square on odd integers. The agent concluded and submitted only the constant-zero and n^2 cases, excluding the piecewise family entirely.
failure_modes.evidence_quote: f(n) = 0 for all n in Z

counterfactual.X: Write answer.txt listing both ground-truth families: the even/odd piecewise form (0 on evens, arbitrary perfect square on odds) and f(n)=n^2 for all n.
counterfactual.Y: Wrote only "f(n) = 0 for all n in Z" and "f(n) = n^2 for all n in Z" as the complete solution set.

failure_modes.name: Budget exhausted before confirmation
failure_modes.description: The sole agent LLM call consumed ~555s of the 600s agent timeout (17268 completion tokens of reasoning) before emitting shell commands; harbor then raised AgentTimeoutError during the next LLM interaction while the harness asked for task-complete confirmation.
failure_modes.evidence_quote: Agent execution timed out after 600.0 seconds

reward_hacking.evidence: The agent performed in-trajectory functional-equation reasoning and wrote a good-faith mathematical answer; no evidence of reading ground_truths.json, bypassing tests, or gaming the verifier.

issues: Grading depends on an LLM-judge ensemble (llm_verifier) rather than a deterministic symbolic checker, so pass/fail can vary with judge stochasticity.
The reference ideal_answer includes a non-obvious piecewise infinite family; agents that find only f(n)=n^2 and constant zero are a common partial-failure pattern.
task_quality.structural_hackability_notes: llm_judge.py scores by prompting external models to compare predicted text to ideal_answer; a model could overfit phrasing to the rubric without a correct proof, and ensemble averaging does not enforce formal equivalence.

aggregated_from.note: Failure modes: union of all judge×round audits, deduped by (A,B,C,D). Outcome/verdicts: majority vote (step_where_lost = median). Individual passes are kept under `passes`. Step indices remapped from the audits' 1-indexing to the viewer's 0-indexing.
